How to use this map−
What this shows. Financial accountability means the ways the public can watch and check the flow of public and private money — central banks, budgets, taxation, procurement, sovereign wealth, financial regulators, company and beneficial-ownership registers, and the audit, anti-corruption and transparency bodies that check how money and power interact.

Coverage. The map holds ~5,600 verified sources. Completeness is measured two ways. Realistic % is the share of the institutions that actually exist with a public website — it excludes bodies a country simply doesn’t have (a sovereign wealth fund or extractives agency where there is none) and localities with no distinct money body. Against that realistic ceiling the map is roughly 86% complete internationally, effectively complete nationally (~100%) and 77% at state level, and far lower at county and municipal level — not because sources are missing, but because most of the world’s ~500,000 municipalities have no separate money-accountability office to list, and local government money is almost entirely one lens (spending). Raw % — the share of every theoretically-possible slot — is therefore near-zero at the local tiers and is only a floor, not a real gap. This map plots those sources for every country — and for every state, county or municipality where data exists — alongside banks, central banks, tax offices, audit offices, stock exchanges, currency-exchange and money-transfer outlets, insurers and accountants worldwide.

Navigate. Click a country to zoom in — its first-level regions load and any that hold sources light up. Click a lit region to open a list of its finance ministries, central-bank branches, audit bodies, regulators and other sources, and to drill deeper into its sub-divisions (state → county → municipal). Click empty space or zoom out to step back up. Facility dots become clickable once you zoom in (about zoom 9); while zoomed out, a click always selects the country or region beneath them.

● banks, central banks, tax, govt finance, audit, exchanges, FX, remittance, insurance, accountants, dev banks, mints — zoom in to click a dot for its details (toggle off, top-right, to select regions freely).

Two ways to filter:
1· Lens pills (right) — each lens is a kind of accountability (officials, agencies, directives, oversight…). Selecting one filters the map to that theme: only countries and regions that actually hold a source of that type stay highlighted, the rest dim, so you can see coverage and gaps at a glance. Below the pills you can narrow further by source type, trust, media and voice.
2· This index (below) — the full directory of every source on the map. Group & sort it with the two drop-downs (by country, level, lens, source type…) and search by name in the filter box.
🌐 Global Wire
The financial climate — a standing overview, with a live wire beneath drawn from a rotating selection of the world’s leading accountability news sources (not all of them).
Before ~1700. Wherever there was treasure there was reckoning — granary tallies, tribute rolls and temple ledgers from Mesopotamia and Egypt to China and the Andes. Rulers taxed and spent as they willed, but the very act of counting wealth planted a seed: what can be counted can one day be questioned.

1700s–1800s. The machinery of public money took shape — treasuries, central banks, national debts and the first audit courts meant to check the sovereign’s accounts. Public borrowing built states and empires alike; the same institutions that promised sound money also financed conquest and slavery, and kept their books far from ordinary eyes.

1800s–1945. Finance globalised — gold standards, stock exchanges, income taxes, and the first company registers and securities laws written after each crash and panic. Yet the age also perfected money as an instrument of domination: colonial extraction, war financing and economies bent to the state, ending in regimes that plundered whole peoples under cover of law.

1945–2000s. The postwar order built the scaffolding still standing today — the IMF and World Bank, national audit institutions almost everywhere, tax authorities and financial regulators, and — as scandal followed scandal — anti-corruption agencies, freedom-of-information laws and the first international rules against bribery and money laundering. Money became traceable in principle, if seldom in practice.

Today (2010s–now). Two currents run against each other. One is transparency’s advance: registers of who really owns companies, automatic exchange of tax data, open budgets and procurement, and leaks — from offshore troves to cross-border investigations — that no jurisdiction can fully contain. The other is evasion’s: shell companies, secrecy jurisdictions, sanctioned finance and crypto rails that move value faster than any watchdog. The meter reads contest, not resolution — more is disclosed than ever, and more is hidden than ever.

Veins to watch. The capture rarely announces itself; it arrives as technical “reforms” and “efficiencies” that each sound reasonable alone. Watch the budget: spending moved off-book into state firms, extra-budgetary funds and “emergency” accounts beyond the normal vote (a disaster used to suspend the rules), supplementaries and reallocations that rewrite priorities after approval, and hidden debt, guarantees and contingent liabilities that surface only in a crisis (Mozambique’s secret “tuna-bond” loans). Watch the audit: supreme audit institutions starved of funds, narrowed in mandate or stripped of the power to sanction, auditors-general replaced with loyalists or left vacant, and findings that name names quietly shelved before they reach the legislature (a public-accounts committee that never sits). Watch procurement: competition engineered away through no-bid, “emergency” and single-source contracting, tailored specifications and split tenders (one bidder for a public deal), winning firms whose real owners hide behind shells, and the revolving door between those who write contracts and those who win them. Watch the money authorities: central-bank independence overridden to fund deficits or prop up a currency before a vote (the printing press as campaign tool), regulators captured by the firms they supervise or defunded into paper tigers, and forbearance that lets favoured banks hide losses until they fall onto the public (Wirecard, waved through by its own watchdog). Watch the tax system: holidays, carve-outs and special zones written for the connected (a statute that fits one company), enforcement aimed at small payers and waved through at the top, and havens, treaty-shopping and transfer mispricing that move the base offshore (profit booked where no factory stands). Watch ownership and disclosure: beneficial-ownership registers left empty, unverified or sealed, officials’ asset declarations unchecked, unpublished or quietly repealed, and the shells, nominees and trusts built to break the trail (a minister’s mansion held by an anonymous offshore entity). Watch the cross-border seam: illicit flows, trade misinvoicing and sanctioned money routed through correspondent banks and free ports, leaks whose aftermath punishes the messenger while the named walk (the whistleblower charged, the banker not), and passports and “golden visas” sold to those fleeing scrutiny elsewhere. Watch enforcement and its mirror: anti-corruption agencies, financial-intelligence units and inspectorates captured, defunded or aimed only at rivals (a graft body that charges only the opposition), cases pressed against opponents and dropped for allies, and impunity engineered through immunities, expired limitation periods, self-amnesties and no-admission settlements that buy a name out of the record (a fine paid, no wrongdoing admitted). And watch the watchers: the boards and councils meant to keep all of this honest — audit boards, regulator and central-bank boards, procurement authorities, sovereign-fund trustees — packed with the very interests they exist to check (a sovereign fund looted by the people who ran it, as in 1MDB). Any one of these can carry an innocent explanation. Where several appear together, and especially where they all move the same way within a single term of office, the public’s money is usually being captured from the inside.
